facts.text: The Bible is a collection of sacred texts rather than a single book written at one time.
The word 'Bible' comes from the Greek ta biblia, meaning 'the books.'
The Christian Bible is divided into the Old Testament and the New Testament.
Most of the Old Testament was written in Hebrew, with some portions in Aramaic.
The New Testament was originally written in Koine Greek.
The Protestant Bible contains 66 books, while Catholic and Orthodox canons include additional books.
The Book of Psalms is the longest book in the Bible by number of chapters.
Psalm 119 is the longest chapter in the Bible.
John 11:35 — 'Jesus wept' — is famously the shortest verse in many English Bible translations.
Genesis 1:3 contains the famous creation line, 'Let there be light.'
David begins Psalm 23 with one of the Bible's best-known lines: 'The Lord is my shepherd; I shall not want.'
In Joshua 1:9, God tells Joshua, 'Be strong and of a good courage,' a verse many readers turn to for encouragement.
Jesus says in Matthew 5:9, 'Blessed are the peacemakers,' during the Sermon on the Mount.
In John 14:6, Jesus says, 'I am the way, the truth, and the life,' one of the most quoted lines in the New Testament.
Jesus tells his disciples in John 13:34, 'Love one another; as I have loved you.'
Paul writes in Philippians 4:13, 'I can do all things through Christ which strengtheneth me.'
1 Corinthians 13 is famous for Paul's teaching on love, including the line 'Charity suffereth long, and is kind.'
Micah 6:8 is often quoted for its call to 'do justly, and to love mercy, and to walk humbly with thy God.'
Proverbs 3:5 says, 'Trust in the Lord with all thine heart,' a line traditionally linked to Solomon's wisdom teaching.
Ecclesiastes 3:1 opens with the memorable words, 'To every thing there is a season, and a time to every purpose under the heaven.'
Galatians 5:22-23 records Paul's 'fruit of the Spirit' list, including love, joy, peace, and self-control.
Isaiah responds to God's call with the famous words, 'Here am I; send me.' in Isaiah 6:8.
The Bible has been translated more widely than any other book in history.
The Dead Sea Scrolls, discovered beginning in 1947, include some of the oldest known manuscripts of Hebrew Bible texts.
The Gutenberg Bible, printed around 1455, was one of the first major books produced with movable type in Europe.
The Septuagint is an ancient Greek translation of the Hebrew Scriptures begun several centuries before the time of Christ.
The Latin Vulgate, translated largely by Jerome in the late 4th century, shaped Western Christianity for centuries.
Chapter divisions used in many modern Bibles were standardized in the Middle Ages, while verse numbers were added later.
William Tyndale's English translation work in the 1500s strongly influenced later English Bibles, including the King James Version.
The King James Version was first published in 1611 and became one of the most influential works in the history of the English language.
Many common English phrases such as 'the powers that be' and 'by the skin of your teeth' became widely known through Bible translations.
The Geneva Bible, published in 1560, was popular among English Protestants and the early American colonists.
The first complete English Bible printed in America was the Aitken Bible in 1782.
Codex Sinaiticus and Codex Vaticanus are among the oldest and most important surviving biblical manuscripts.
Biblical books were written across many centuries by numerous authors, editors, and communities.
The four Gospels in the New Testament are Matthew, Mark, Luke, and John.
The Apostle Paul's letters, or epistles, make up a large portion of the New Testament.
The Book of Genesis opens with the creation narrative and early patriarchal history.
The Book of Exodus centers on Moses, the liberation of the Israelites from Egypt, and the giving of the law.
The Book of Revelation is the final book of the New Testament and is known for its vivid apocalyptic imagery.
The Bible has inspired countless works of art, music, literature, law, and political speech throughout history.
Different religious traditions arrange biblical books in slightly different orders and canons.
Modern study Bibles often include maps, notes, cross-references, and historical background to help readers understand the text.
Genesis 1:1 opens with the words, "In the beginning God created the heaven and the earth."
That opening line is one of the best-known starting passages in all of scripture.
Genesis 1:1 is one of the standout topics people mention when discussing The Bible.
Any overview of The Bible usually includes Genesis 1:1.
Genesis 1:1 helped define the legacy and reputation of The Bible.
Fans exploring The Bible are often encouraged to learn about Genesis 1:1.
The story of The Bible is hard to explain without mentioning Genesis 1:1.
Retrospectives and fan discussions about The Bible frequently return to Genesis 1:1.
For many fans, Genesis 1:1 captures part of why The Bible stays so memorable.
Lists, articles, and guides about The Bible often highlight Genesis 1:1.

Genesis is the first book of the Bible and includes creation stories, the flood, and the patriarchs.
It serves as one of the main starting points for readers exploring the Bible.
Genesis is one of the standout topics fans often explore within The Bible.
Readers and viewers interested in The Bible often come across Genesis very quickly.
Learning about Genesis gives a better feel for why The Bible remains so memorable.
Genesis remains one of the best-known reference points connected with The Bible.
Articles, rankings, and fan discussions about The Bible regularly highlight Genesis.
For many people, Genesis is an easy entry point into the wider world of The Bible.
The story of Genesis helps explain part of the lasting appeal of The Bible.
Genesis is frequently mentioned in retrospectives and guides about The Bible.
One reason The Bible stays fun to revisit is the presence of major touchpoints like Genesis.
Genesis still stands out as a classic example from The Bible.
